返回日报
Hacker News #5

大多数争论都是关于自我，而不是想法

Most arguments are about ego, not ideas

AI 作者 backlit4034 热度 711 回复 557 2026/07/01 21:29
打开原帖 打开外部链接 JSON

摘要

  • I am a software engineer, and I used to enjoy arguing with people for technical correctness. Code reviews, design meetings, mailing-list threads, dinner tables. If someone was wrong, I wanted them to know it, and I wanted them to know exactly why. I collected counterarguments the way I collected patches. I believed that if I just laid out the logic clearly enough, the other person would have no choice but to come around. Truth would win.
  • It almost never worked that way.

命中 1 个 AI/科技关键词。

正文翻译

暂无正文

暂无原文

回复翻译 20

a4isms 2026/07/01 21:59
这里有一个简单的想法：你不能说服某人离开他们没有推理自己的立场。

需要考虑三种解释：

0：默认值：那个人非理性地认为自己是错误的。最好走开，争论是徒劳的，我还有生活要过。

1：哇！有时那个人就是我。

2：如果他们没有说服自己，那么他们是如何陷入的？如果他们的立场代表了他们的价值观，而不是为了最大化某些假设的正确性而制定的完美策略，该怎么办？那么，如果我想和他们讨论，我应该谈论他们的价值观和我的价值观以及它们的交叉点，而不是争论对错？

我个人发现上述所有三个在某一点或另一点都很有用。

Here's a simple idea: You can't reason someone out of a position they didn't reason themselves into.

And three interpretations to consider:

0: The default: That person is irrationally attached to being wrong. Best to walk away, argumentation will be futile, and I have a life to lead.

1: Whoa! Sometimes that person is me.

2: If they didn't reason themselves into it, how did they get into it? What if their position represents their values, not some perfectly architected strategy for maximizing some hypothetical measure of rightness? In that case, if I wish to discuss it with them, I should be talking about their values and my values and where they intersect, rather than arguing right and wrong?

I have personally found all three of the above useful at one point or anther.

楼层
Supermancho 2026/07/01 22:13
我的在线参与风格由两个想法决定：

1.我很少完全理解自己对细节的立场 2. 写作就是重写。

我写论坛帖子是为了巩固我对自己的兴趣、信仰和推理的理解。我经常在继续之前对其进行多次编辑，然后忽略此后的回复。我可以参考它们，也可以参考其他询问我意见的人。有时我会立即回复回复，有时我会在几天后，在我有时间将其融入日常背景后再次访问。这并不是一条硬性规定。

也许从 20 年前（大约 30 岁）起，发帖就不再是为了说服别人了。我发帖是为了回顾并了解自己。对于其他人来说，我确信这听起来像是存在主义的自我中心主义和以自我为中心的胡言乱语，但我不介意。

My style of online participation has been shaped by 2 ideas:

1. I rarely fully understand my own positions on minutia 2. Writing is rewriting.

I write forum posts to solidify my understanding of my own interests, beliefs, and reasoning. I often edit them multiple times before moving on and ignoring the responses thereafter. I can reference them and have to other people who ask my opinion. Sometimes I do respond back to replies immediately, and sometimes I revisit days later, after I've had time to put it in my day-to-day context. It's not a hard and fast rule.

Posting stopped being about convincing someone else maybe 20 years ago (around age 30). I do post to look back and understand myself. To others, I'm sure this sounds like existential navel-gazing and self-centered blathering, but I don't mind.

楼层
sejje 2026/07/01 22:22
我也做了同样的事情，除了大部分时间我删除了回复。写作对我来说很重要，但阅读对其他人来说并不重要。这会浪费他们的时间。

我猜大约 40% 的评论都是我发表的。

I do the same, except mostly I delete the responses. The writing was important to me, but the reading is rarely important to someone else. It would be wasting their time.

I would guess I post about 40% of the comments I write.

楼层
lelandfe 2026/07/01 22:34
林肯以写信而不是邮寄信件作为发泄情绪的方式而闻名。
Lincoln famously wrote and never mailed letters as a way to vent emotions.
楼层
EvanAnderson 2026/07/01 23:03
这对我来说太奇怪了，这竟然有效，在我尝试之前我不能说我相信它。我不应该对欺骗我们的大脑如此容易感到惊讶。

我认为这样的现象是同样的欺骗的一个方面：我写了一封电子邮件的 90%，将其保存为草稿以便稍后完成，却永远不记得完成它，被问及它并无可辩驳地确定我已发送它，然后最终发现它是未完成的草稿。愚蠢的大脑...... Grrr。

It's so bizarre to me that this works and I can't say I believed it until I tried. I shouldn't be surprised that it's so easy to trick our brains.

I assume the phenomenon where I write 90% of an email, save it as a draft to finish later, never remember to finish it, get asked about it and have irrefutable certainty I sent it, then finally discover it as an unfinished draft is a facet of the same trickery. Stupid brain... Grrr.

楼层
Nevermark 2026/07/02 06:53
我认为争论的驱动因素之一是我们试图理解某些东西，而争论在潜意识中不仅是抨击他们的想法，而且是测试我们自己的力量的一种方式。

在努力创建书面回应时，我们：

1.发现要明确我们的观点并很好地描述它们需要比我们想象的更多的努力。

2.为了使清晰的描述成为可能，我们常常需要比我们预期更多地调整自己的想法。实现“更好地了解自己”几乎总是一种令人安心的委婉说法，意为“解决我们自己令人惊讶的欠发达/不连贯的想法”。

3。这会让心情变得更加谦虚。

4.意识到问题比我们想象的更加微妙，并更好地评估对话的深度，即使是最具建设性的参与也需要如此。

5.意识到我们没有精力去做这件事。

6。对将我们的想法付诸测试并“更好地理解它们”感到高兴。所以这是一个停下来的好地方。

争论（在战斗中测试我们的想法）的实际需要是真实的，我们只是不需要其他人进行争论。他们想象中的阻力迫使我们改进我们的思维，可能比我们承认的还要多。

在准备比赛时，任何人能做的最好的事情就是告诉你你的对手取得了比他们更好的成绩。通过与一个想象中的竞争对手竞争，你会获得更多的收益，因为他总是能看到你所有的弱点，而现实中的竞争对手可能不会看到你的弱点。

I think one of the drivers of arguments is we are trying to understand something, and the argument is subconsciously a way to not just bash their ideas, but test the strength of our own.

In the effort to create a written response we:

1. Find it takes more effort than we imagined to nail down our views, and describe them well.

2. Often have to adapt our own ideas more than we expected to make a clear description possible. Achieving a "better understanding of ourselves" is almost always a reassuring euphemism for "fixing our own surprisingly under-developed/under-coherent ideas".

3. This puts in a more humble mood.

4. Realize the issue is more nuanced than we imagine, and better assess the depth of conversation that even the most constructive engagement would require.

5. Realize we don't have energy left for that.

6. Feel good about having put our ideas to the test and "understanding them better". So a good place to stop.

The practical need to argue (test our ideas in battle) was real, we just didn't need the other person to have the argument. Their imagined pushback forced us to improve our thinking, probably more than we acknowledge.

When preparing for a race, the best thing anyone can do is tell you your opponent is achieving better times than they are. You will achieve more gains by competing with an imaginary competitor, who always sees all your weaknesses, than a real one who likely would not.

楼层
t-writescode 2026/07/02 03:03
写日记这事儿我以前觉得挺傻的，直到我亲自试了试；然后脑海里就开始不断上演“你真的这么想吗？”、“这是真的吗？”、“那这个呢？”还有“你干嘛要对着一张纸撒谎？”这样的自我拷问，我这才恍然大悟：“哦，我懂了。”

仔细想想，这正是AI全自主编程无法引起我共鸣，或者让我觉得没有成长和学习的原因。我短路掉了“写日记”这个步骤，也就是在脑海中审视和抨击自己想法与假设的过程。

Journaling sounded stupid to me, until I tried it; and then the whole “do you really think that?”, “is that true?”, “what about this?” and “why are you lying to a sheet of paper?” started happening and I was like, “oh I get it.”

Come to think of it, that’s a major reason why fully agentic coding doesn’t resonate with me and/or feels like I’m not growing or learning. I’m short-circuiting the “journaling” step where I mentally attack my own thoughts and assumptions.

楼层
cvwright 2026/07/02 06:39
在让AI写代码之前，你应该先试着和它一起写设计文档。它会根据你的第一个提示词进行各种随机推演，有好有坏。然后你就有机会在机器人的辅助下，和自己展开一场来回辩论。
You should try writing a design doc with the AI before you have it write the code. It will make random extrapolations from your first prompt, some good and some bad. Then you get a chance to argue back and forth with yourself with the robot as a helper.
楼层
t-writescode 2026/07/02 08:17
哦，太对了！我发现一只爱抬杠的“橡皮鸭”是AI对我而言最棒的用途之一！
Oh yes! I find an argumentative rubber duck to be one of the best uses of AI for myself!
楼层
JackFr 2026/07/02 06:58
“规格说明中是否存在任何歧义、未决问题或隐藏假设？请深入思考。”
“Are there any ambiguities, open questions, or hidden assumptions in spec? Please think deeply.”
楼层
unselect5917 2026/07/02 03:36
我给我那无能的租赁办公室写了一封电子邮件，主要是为了发泄情绪，然后还是将其发送出去，这样我就可以收到证据，证明他们已经以书面形式阅读了该邮件，以应对未来的任何问题。这是相当宣泄的。
I wrote an email to my titanically incompetent leasing office mostly to vent emotions, then sent it anyway so I could receive proof that they had read it in writing for any future issues. It was quite cathartic.

throw0101a 2026/07/01 22:45
> 我经常在继续之前对其进行多次编辑，然后忽略此后的回复。

“抱歉，这封信太长了，我没有时间缩短它。”

* https://quoteinvestigator.com/2012/04/28/shorter-letter/

> I often edit them multiple times before moving on and ignoring the responses thereafter.

"Sorry this letter is so long as I did not have time to make it shorter."

* https://quoteinvestigator.com/2012/04/28/shorter-letter/

楼层
pbronez 2026/07/02 01:00
1000% 这个。我写作是为了强迫自己深入思考并具体化我对事物的看法。
1000% this. I write to force myself to think deeply and crystallize my opinion on things.
楼层
adverbly 2026/07/01 23:21
> 如果他们的立场代表了他们的价值观怎么办

我最好的教授之一经常问我：

“你想在这里实现什么？”

每次他们问这个问题时，总是让我陷入深度思考模式。在某些情况下，它确实会引发防御性思维，但我认为，如果你想改变对某件事的想法，就必须退后一步并深入思考来实际参与，这是最好的选择。

> What if their position represents their values

One of my best professors often asked me:

"what are you trying to achieve here?"

Every time they asked this, it always put me into a deep thinking mode. In some cases it did trigger defensive mindsets, but I think having to actually engage by taking a step back and think deeply is for the best if you want to have any hope of changing your mind on something.

楼层
JackFr 2026/07/02 07:04
“我能做些什么来解决这个问题？”

“嗯。我无法回到过去，但如果你愿意，我可以静静地坐着，带着悔恨的表情，听你对我辱骂 10 分钟。这样有效吗？”

“What can I do to resolve this?”

“Hmmm. I cannot go back in time, but if you’d like I can sit quietly with a contrite look while you spew abuse at me for 10 minutes. Does that work?”

楼层
pseudohadamard 2026/07/02 10:49
在某些情况下，即使您知道自己无法获胜，也值得争论，以抵制真正愚蠢的想法。这种情况在标准化工作中还远远不够，少数专业会议参与者可能会通过令人难以置信的糟糕、破碎的标准，因为没有人反对。

任何曾经在标准机构工作过的人都会在这一点上回想起。

In some cases it's worth arguing even if you know you can't win in order to provide pushback against really dumb ideas. This doesn't happen nearly enough in standardisation work, where a small number of professional meeting-goers can railroad through unbelievably bad, broken standards because no-one pushes back.

Anyone who's ever worked on a standards body will be getting flashbacks at this point.

楼层
anthonypasq 2026/07/01 22:05
> 你无法说服某人脱离他们没有说服自己的立场。

这是一个精辟的想法，但事实并非如此，每个失去宗教信仰并被理性论证说服的人都是一个反例。

> You can't reason someone out of a position they didn't reason themselves into.

this is a pithy think to say but its really not true, and every person that has lost their religion and been convinced by rational argument is a counter example.

楼层
throw0101a 2026/07/01 22:46
> 这是一个精辟的想法，但事实并非如此，每个失去宗教信仰并被理性论证说服的人都是一个反例。

那些被理性论证说服上帝必须存在的人呢？对于某些人（亚里士多德、普罗提诺、莱布尼茨等）来说，否认这种存在是不合理的：

* https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/35592365-five-proofs-of-...

你似乎还暗示理性是一个整体：

* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whose_Justice%3F_Which_Rationa...

> this is a pithy think to say but its really not true, and every person that has lost their religion and been convinced by rational argument is a counter example.

And what of people that were convinced by rational argument that a God must exist? To some (Aristotle, Plotinus, Leibniz, etc) it is irrational to deny such existence:

* https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/35592365-five-proofs-of-...

You also seem to imply that rationality is a single monolithic thing:

* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whose_Justice%3F_Which_Rationa...

楼层
al_borland 2026/07/01 23:29
人们真的会因为逻辑和理性而失去宗教信仰吗？我从来没有见过这个。通常还有一些更深层次的故事。如果有人问我为什么不相信，尽管我是在教堂长大的，我会简单地说这没有道理，如果他们坚持的话，我会喋喋不休地讲道理和逻辑。这只是一个盾牌，以避免与我在深度上不信任的人分享真相。
Do people really lose their religion because of logic and reason? I’ve never seen this. There is usually some deeper story. If someone asks me why I don’t believe, despite being raised in the church, I’ll simply say it didn’t make sense and babble on about reason and logic if they push. This is just a shield to avoid sharing the truth with people I don’t trust on a very deep level.
楼层
